title: TCP/IP Network Access Layer
description: Combines OSI layers 1 and 2 — handles physical transmission and local network framing.
fullExplanation: The TCP/IP Network Access layer (also called the Link layer) combines what OSI separates into Physical (L1) and Data Link (L2) layers. It handles everything needed to transmit data over a single physical link — from the electrical signals on copper wire to the Ethernet frame format and MAC addressing. This layer is network-technology specific: Ethernet, WiFi, PPP, and DSL all operate here but have different frame formats and addressing schemes.

protocols.name: Ethernet (802.3)
protocols.description: Dominant wired LAN — MAC addresses, CRC, CSMA/CD

protocols.name: ARP
protocols.description: Address Resolution Protocol — maps IP to MAC address

protocols.name: PPP
protocols.description: Point-to-Point Protocol — WAN links, dial-up, DSL

protocols.name: DSL
protocols.description: Digital Subscriber Line — broadband over phone lines

protocols.name: 802.1Q (VLAN)
protocols.description: Virtual LAN tagging on Ethernet frames

keyFacts: MAC addresses (48-bit) used for local delivery within a single network segment
ARP bridges Internet layer (IP) and Network Access layer (MAC)
Ethernet MTU: 1500 bytes — IP fragmentation occurs if packet exceeds this
Network Access layer PDU is called a 'Frame'
NIC operates at this layer — hardware address burned into EEPROM
Switches operate at this layer — forward frames based on MAC table
Unlike OSI, TCP/IP doesn't specify what goes here — any link technology works
